items.title: Apple Is Suing OpenAI for Allegedly Stealing Hardware Secrets
items.summary: The iPhone maker claims OpenAI encouraged poached employees to bring over confidential presentations, secret prototypes, and key supplier details.

items.title: Meet the Battery Startup Taking on China’s Giants
items.summary: Solid-state batteries are safer and more capable—but harder to mass-produce. They also represent an opportunity for non-Chinese companies to get back in the game.

items.title: What Watching a Soccer Final Does to Your Body, According to Science
items.summary: A recent study tracked hundreds of soccer fans until their favorite team reached the final of a tournament. Their stress levels skyrocketed, and their heart rates jumped too.

items.title: Bluesky’s interim CEO, Toni Schneider, drops the ‘interim’
items.summary: Schneider, who formerly served as the CEO of Automattic and is a partner at True Ventures, says he is "all in" on the unconventional social media platform.

items.title: Apple sues OpenAI over alleged trade secret theft
items.summary: Apple alleges the misconduct was directed by OpenAi's senior leadership, including a long-time former employee.

items.title: Filing: College app Fizz accuses VC of sharing confidential startup information with rival Sidechat
items.summary: Fizz has expanded its lawsuit against rival Sidechat, alleging that a Maveron VC shared its confidential information obtained during a fundraising meeting with the competing startup.

items.title: Wildfire in southern Spain leaves at least 12 dead and 23 missing
items.summary: A local official said four of the victims may be British after a fire broke out near Los Gallardos, Almería.

items.title: East Asia braces for destructive typhoon as landslides kill 15 in Philippines
items.summary: Heading for Taiwan and south-eastern China, the 1,000 km-wide Bavi is forecast to be one of the strongest storms in decades.

items.title: Zulu king expresses regret after video captures tirade against his wife
items.summary: In the footage widely shared in South Africa, King Misuzulu threatens to physically assault his wife and accuses her of cheating.

items.title: Kevin Warsh names members of his Federal Reserve task forces, including Marc Andreessen, Doug McMillon
items.summary: Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh on Thursday released names of the experts who will comprise five task forces to examine the institution's operations.

items.title: Prediction markets spark insider trading concerns. Here's how Goldman and other companies are responding
items.summary: CNBC reached out to 50 companies about what are their trading policies for employees on prediction markets. A handful had an answer.

items.title: Kalshi traders think gas prices will stay higher for longer as U.S.-Iran tensions heat back up
items.summary: Odds that gas prices are above $3.50 on Election Day have jumped to 75% on the platform.

items.title: Stocks Sink in Broad AI Rout Sparked by China's DeepSeek
items.summary: U.S. stocks were mostly lower, with the Nasdaq leading declines as makers of AI infrastructure suffered steep falls, many in the double digits. Nvidia was down 16%.

items.title: Comex Gold, Silver Settle Lower
items.summary: Gold settled 1.4% lower, down two of the past three sessions, and silver fell 2.5%, down three of the past four sessions.

items.title: DeepSeek Won't Sink U.S. AI Titans
items.summary: Panic fueling the selloff of Nvidia, Broadcom and other tech giants is overblown.

items.title: Fears erupt over Iran’s desire to kill Trump
items.summary: A reported new threat by Iran to assassinate President Trump served as a reminder this week of the risk that the hard-line Tehran regime poses to him, especially as a tenuous ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran breaks down and hostilities have resumed. Reports surfaced this week that Israel shared intelligence with the U.S. indicating…

items.title: UK, Norway embassies get cheeky in social media post over Haaland, Kane World Cup rivalry
items.summary: The British and Norwegian embassies in Washington shared some light-hearted banter on social media ahead of the match between England and Norway on Saturday in the quarterfinals of the FIFA World Cup. The post centered on the friendly rivalry between Norwegian star Erling Haaland and English player Harry Kane. Haaland is also a striker for…

items.title: Platner officially withdraws from Maine Senate race
items.summary: Democrat Graham Platner has formally withdrawn from the Maine Senate race, allowing members of the party to choose their new nominee this month. “I write to formally withdraw my candidacy for United States Senate,” the progressive oyster farmer wrote in a letter posted to social media Friday, later adding that “my name may have been…

items.title: AI Infrastructure Stock Gets Big Price-Target Hike After Acquisition
items.summary: AI Infrastructure Stock Gets Big Price-Target Hike After Acquisition

items.title: The biggest problem with solid-state batteries may finally be solved
items.summary: Researchers solved the mystery of how soft lithium dendrites crack the hard ceramic inside solid-state batteries, triggering short circuits. The breakthrough could help engineers build safer, longer-lasting batteries for smartphones, electric vehicles, and other electronics.

items.title: The galaxy’s coldest “stars” may actually be alien megastructures
items.summary: Scientists have identified new clues that could help astronomers spot one of the most famous hypothetical alien megastructures: a Dyson sphere. The study finds that red dwarfs and white dwarfs are the most promising stars to examine, since advanced civilizations could potentially build energy-harvesting swarms around them more easily. These objects would stand out by glowing in infrared light instead of visible light, lacking the dusty signatures of ordinary stars, and possibly flickering in unusual ways.

items.title: This alien planet never has sunrise or sunset. It may support life
items.summary: A planet with one side permanently roasting and the other frozen in endless darkness might still have a chance of supporting life. Researchers found that heat inside a tidally locked exoplanet could circulate in a stable, continuous loop, helping moderate temperatures in certain regions. Their laboratory model suggests these worlds may be more hospitable than previously thought, despite their extreme surface conditions.

items.title: Pluto has landslides
items.summary: New Horizons data reveal Pluto’s first six confirmed landslides along steep crater rims.

items.title: Here’s what happens when you put politicians in charge of science
items.summary: Proposed federal rules would rely on political appointees to decide how a lot of U.S. science gets done. History shows the consequences of such actions.

items.title: Lost in Curiosity reveals the messy reality of doing science
items.summary: In her new book, science journalist Roberta Kwok takes readers behind the scenes to understand how researchers get nature to give up its secrets.
